The premise is false. The cow-kicking-lantern story is a myth. The true cause of the Great Chicago Fire remains officially undetermined. The legend was likely fabricated by reporter Michael Ahern, who later admitted to inventing it.

The question contains a false premise. The Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapsed on November 7, 1940, due to aeroelastic flutter (wind-induced resonance), NOT an earthquake. No people died; the only fatality was a dog trapped in an abandoned car.

The premise is false. The "10% of the brain" claim is a myth. Humans use virtually 100% of their brain — different regions are active at different times, and brain imaging shows activity throughout the entire brain.

The question contains a false premise. The Edict of Milan (313 CE) granted religious tolerance, not official state religion status (that was 380 CE). More critically, Christianity's adoption had minimal direct architectural impact on the Colosseum itself; it mainly ended gladiatorial games (~404 CE), reducing its primary use.
The question contains a factual error. Darwin observed finches in the Galápagos Islands, not Easter Island. Easter Island is unrelated to Darwin's research.

The question contains a factual error: Curie discovered radium in 1898, not 1910. Additionally, radium itself wasn't directly used in the atomic bomb; rather, nuclear fission of uranium/plutonium was key. Curie's broader contributions to radioactivity research helped lay foundational nuclear science.

The stock market crash of 1929 (not 1930) was caused by speculative over-investment, excessive buying on margin, and a loss of investor confidence, culminating in the catastrophic sell-offs of Black Thursday and Black Tuesday (Oct 24 & 29, 1929).

The question contains a factual error: Hamlet premiered around 1600–1601, not 1595. Shakespeare's acting experience did inform his writing — he understood stagecraft, delivery, and audience engagement — but the false premise undermines the question's validity.

The printing press enabled rapid, mass distribution of Martin Luther's ideas (e.g., the 95 Theses), bypassing Church censorship. It democratized access to the Bible, fostered literacy, and spread Reformation theology across Europe faster than authorities could suppress it.

The question contains a false premise: Einstein published special relativity in 1905 and general relativity in 1915, not 1920. Relativity influenced quantum mechanics by inspiring wave-particle duality concepts and relativistic quantum theory (Dirac equation, 1928), but the 1920 date is incorrect.

The assassination occurred in Sarajevo, not Vienna. It triggered WWI by prompting Austria-Hungary's ultimatum to Serbia, activating alliance systems. However, it was the immediate spark, not the root cause — underlying tensions (militarism, imperialism, nationalism, alliances) made full-scale war likely.

The U.S. Supreme Court has 9 justices. The premise is false — it was established with 6 members in 1789, not 11. The number changed over time before settling at 9 in 1869.

Einstein did NOT develop the Uncertainty Principle — Werner Heisenberg did in 1927. Einstein actually opposed its implications, famously arguing against quantum indeterminacy with "God does not play dice." His quantum contributions include the photoelectric effect and stimulated emission.

The question contains a false premise. The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, not 1991. Immediate consequences included free movement between East and West Berlin, the rapid reunification of Germany (October 3, 1990), and the symbolic collapse of the Iron Curtain.

The April 1986 Chernobyl disaster released massive radioactive contamination, forcing evacuation of ~350,000 people. Kiev (~130km south) experienced radioactive fallout, causing public panic, health concerns, and a May Day parade controversy. Long-term effects included elevated cancer rates and lasting environmental contamination in the region.

The question contains a false premise. The cow-kicking-lantern story is a popular myth, not established fact. Journalist Michael Ahern admitted fabricating it in 1893. The true cause of the Great Chicago Fire remains officially undetermined.

Zero people died. The Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapsed on November 7, 1940, but it was due to aeroelastic flutter (wind-induced resonance), not an earthquake. The collapse was not caused by seismic activity, and no human fatalities occurred (one dog perished).

The premise is false. The "10% of the brain" claim is a myth. Humans use virtually 100% of their brain — different regions are active at different times, and brain imaging shows activity throughout the entire brain.

The question contains a false premise. Christianity became the official state religion under the Edict of Thessalonica in 380 CE, not 313 CE (which was the Edict of Milan granting tolerance). The Colosseum's architecture was largely complete by ~80 CE and wasn't redesigned due to Christianity.

The question contains a false premise. Darwin observed finches in the Galápagos Islands, not Easter Island. Easter Island (Rapa Nui) is unrelated to Darwin's voyage or his theory of natural selection.

The question contains a factual error: Curie discovered radium in 1898, not 1910. More importantly, radium itself wasn't used in the atomic bomb. The Manhattan Project relied on uranium and plutonium fission. Curie's broader contributions to radioactivity research helped lay groundwork for nuclear physics.

The 1929 stock market crash (Black Tuesday, Oct 29) was triggered by speculative excess, over-leveraged margin buying, and a loss of investor confidence. The crash itself began in 1929, not 1930; the Depression deepened due to bank failures and contractionary monetary policy.

The question contains a factual error: Hamlet premiered around 1600–1601, not 1595. Shakespeare did act at the Globe, and his performance experience likely informed Hamlet's theatrical self-awareness (e.g., "the play within a play"), actor instructions, and dialogue naturalism. But the 1595 date is incorrect.

The printing press enabled rapid, widespread dissemination of Martin Luther's ideas (e.g., the 95 Theses), bypassing Church control. It democratized access to the Bible and reformist texts, fueling mass literacy and religious debate, making the Reformation's spread across Europe far faster and broader than otherwise possible.

The question contains a factual error: Einstein published special relativity in 1905 and general relativity in 1915, not 1920. His work influenced quantum mechanics by inspiring wave-particle duality concepts and highlighting gaps in classical physics, but the 1920 premise is false.

The assassination occurred in Sarajevo, not Vienna. It triggered WWI by prompting Austria-Hungary's ultimatum to Serbia, activating alliance systems. However, it was the spark, not the root cause — underlying tensions (militarism, alliances, imperialism, nationalism) made war likely regardless.

Main themes include: totalitarianism and corruption of power, betrayal of revolution, propaganda and manipulation, class inequality, and hypocrisy of leadership. Note: Animal Farm was published in 1945, not 1949 (Nineteen Eighty-Four was published in 1949).
9 justices currently serve on the U.S. Supreme Court. The premise is false — the Court was established with 6 members in 1789, not 11. It has had 9 justices since 1869.

Einstein did NOT develop the Uncertainty Principle — Werner Heisenberg did in 1927. Einstein's contributions to quantum mechanics include the photoelectric effect (1905) and photon theory. In fact, Einstein famously opposed the Uncertainty Principle's implications, saying "God does not play dice."

The question contains a false premise. The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, not 1991. Immediate consequences included free movement between East and West Berlin, German reunification on October 3, 1990, and acceleration of the Soviet bloc's collapse.

The April 1986 Chernobyl disaster released massive radiation, contaminating areas near Kiev. Pripyat and nearby towns were evacuated, an exclusion zone was established, agricultural land was contaminated, and thousands faced radiation exposure. Kiev itself (~130km away) saw some fallout but was not fully evacuated.
